Bharti Airtel is India's second-largest telecom operator by subscribers (482M India mobile, 184M Africa) and a dominant player in the consolidating Indian wireless market, now also consolidating Indus Towers as a subsidiary. Full-year FY26 revenue grew +21.9% YoY to ₹21,097 cr (quarterly run-rate ₹55,383 cr at +15.7% YoY for Q4FY26 standalone), with EBITDA margins holding firmly at ~58%, while PAT at the annual level fell 9.7% YoY due to a ₹316 cr exceptional regulatory levy charge — the underlying PAT trajectory is positive with Q4FY26 PAT up +38.7% YoY, but the annual figure masks one-off tax headwinds. On valuation, trailing P/E of 41.78× sits at the lower end of the 35–60× telecom sector benchmark, and critically the forward P/E of 22.2× implies substantial earnings growth is expected, while EV/EBITDA of 10.68× looks genuinely inexpensive for a capital-intensive telecom with debt/EBITDA of only 0.94× and OCF/capex of 3.18×, warranting a 'fair-to-cheap' verdict on a through-cycle basis. The single biggest risk is the combination of extremely high financial leverage (Debt/Equity of 99.72×) with capex intensity at 29% of revenue, leaving PAT highly sensitive to interest cost movements and limiting deleveraging pace if ARPU repair stalls.

The key risk is NIM compression: domestic NIM has slipped to 2.93% in Q4, the RBI easing cycle could pressure asset yields further, and the FY27 guidance of 'above 3% NIM' for the full year will require careful liability management to achieve.

At a trailing P/E of 17.69× versus the FMCG sector median of 40–60×, ITC trades at a steep discount to consumer staples peers, though this is structural (cigarette business attracts a sin-stock de-rating) and EV/EBITDA of 13.07× confirms the same cheap absolute reading; the stock sits near its 52-week low at only 10.3% of its range, which improves entry risk-reward but also signals persistent selling pressure — note the dividend yield figure of 530% appears to be a data artefact and should be verified. The single biggest risk is that the February 2026 excise duty increase on cigarettes (the core earnings engine at ~57% segment margin) structurally pressures volumes and pricing power in FY27, compounding the already muted PAT trajectory.

GAIL (India) Limited is the country's dominant natural gas transmission and distribution company, operating the largest pipeline network in India with additional presence in LPG, petrochemicals, and gas marketing. The multi-quarter trend table is largely unpopulated from the PDF extraction, but yfinance data shows Q3FY26 revenue of ₹29,395 cr and PAT of ₹1,756 cr (PAT margin ~6.0%), while Q1FY26 showed PAT of ₹2,369 cr (margin ~8.2%), indicating a meaningful PAT margin compression of roughly 220 bps across two quarters — a deteriorating trajectory even if revenue stayed broadly flat near ₹29,000 cr. On valuation, trailing P/E of 14.7× sits above the Energy sector median of 9.2× (at P75 among peers), and EV/EBITDA of 11.6× is a steep 97% premium to the sector median of 5.9×, making GAIL expensive relative to peers like BPCL and ONGC on the multiples that matter most for a capital-intensive pipeline utility; the forward P/E of 9.5× does imply meaningful earnings growth expected ahead, but this is not yet reflected in confirmed guidance. The biggest risk is that rising crude at $93.65 inflates GAIL's gas sourcing and LPG costs faster than it can pass through to customers, further compressing already-declining PAT margins while debt/equity of 27.8× (likely debt-to-equity in absolute terms given ₹1.1L cr market cap context) keeps interest coverage under scrutiny.

fundamental.thesis: Indian Oil Corporation (IOC) is India's largest downstream oil refiner and fuel marketer, commanding significant market share in refining throughput (75.5 MMT in FY26) and retail fuel distribution. FY26 full-year PAT surged 183.9% YoY to ₹36,802 cr (vs ₹12,962 cr in FY25) and revenue grew a modest 4.8% YoY to ₹8,86,224 cr, with EBITDA margins expanding sharply to 7.4% from a depressed 1.65% base — a genuine recovery year driven by normalised crude procurement costs and operational efficiency, though the Q3-to-full-year PAT margin trajectory appears flat at 4.2%, signalling the recovery may have already peaked. Valuation is unambiguously cheap: trailing P/E of 4.7× is 49% below the sector peer median of 9.2× and well below the Energy sector benchmark range of 8–14×; EV/EBITDA of 4.2× is 25% below the peer median; P/B of 0.9× with ROE of 21% TTM is a classically undervalued combination — IOC ranks P0 (cheapest) in its sector on trailing P/E and P/B. The single biggest risk is that forward P/E of 8.2× exceeds trailing P/E of 4.7×, explicitly signalling the market expects a material earnings decline in FY27 — driven by elevated crude at $105/bbl, INR weakening to 95.96 (inflating import costs), a cumulative LPG under-recovery buffer of ₹23,102 cr, and no government pricing freedom — meaning FY26 PAT was likely a cyclical peak that will not repeat.

Canara Bank is a large public-sector bank with ~₹12.4L cr in advances, ranking among India's top-4 PSBs by loan book; it has been executing a sustained cleanup of its balance sheet while growing its retail lending portfolio aggressively (+32.93% YoY). In FY26, the bank delivered PAT of ₹19,187 cr, up 12.7% YoY on revenue growth of 7.7%, with Gross NPA compressing 110 bps to 1.84%, credit cost improving to 0.59%, and ROA reaching 1.1% — all directionally positive and consistent with management's track record of beating stated guidance (FY26 loan growth of 15.3% vs 10-11% guided). At a trailing P/E of 5.6× against the sector median of 12-18× and a P/B of 1.03× with ROE of 17.6%, the stock is unambiguously cheap on a peer-relative basis (P5 on P/B, P10 on trailing P/E in a 20-stock sector cohort), though forward P/E of 5.81× being fractionally above trailing P/E suggests the market expects modest earnings normalisation ahead. The primary risk is CASA ratio weakness at 29.86% (below the ~35-40% range of stronger private peers), which constrains NIM — guided at a thin 2.5-2.6% for FY27 — and leaves the bank vulnerable to deposit cost pressure if the RBI easing cycle is shallower than expected.

Revenue growth has decelerated modestly from +14.0% (Q3 9M basis) to +10.9% (Q3 standalone YoY), with PAT margin at 42.6% and recruitment segment EBITDA margin at 59%, reflecting a high-quality, asset-light core business — though the trajectory signal is mildly negative as standalone growth lags the 9M average. Trailing P/E of 45× sits near the lower bound of the FMCG/consumer-internet premium range and is at P0 (cheapest) among Consumer Discretionary peers, with P/B of only 1.3× versus a peer median of 9.5×, making the stock look optically cheap — however, ROE of just 4.6% and EV/EBITDA of 52.5× (P80 in sector) temper the value case, as the low P/B reflects capital tied up in listed investments rather than genuine book cheapness. fundamental.thesis: Ashok Leyland is India's second-largest commercial vehicle (CV) manufacturer with strong presence in MHCV and LCV segments, posting an all-time high Q3FY26 consolidated revenue of ₹14,830 cr (+23.7% YoY) and PAT of ₹813 cr (+6.8% YoY), though PAT growth lagging revenue growth signals margin pressure — EBITDA margin held at 13.3% (12th consecutive double-digit quarter) but a one-time ₹331 cr exceptional charge related to new Labour Codes depressed reported PAT margin to 5.5%. Valuation at trailing P/E of 29.1× sits exactly at the sector median for Auto (18–28× benchmark), making it borderline fair-to-slightly-expensive on earnings, but EV/EBITDA of 12.3× is at P22 vs peers (22nd percentile — cheapest quartile), suggesting the capital-intensive CV business is not stretched on an operating-earnings basis; P/B of 7.6× at P88 vs peers is elevated and warrants monitoring. The biggest fundamental risk is the combination of sharply weakening INR (95.96) and elevated crude at $105.42, both of which compress CV operator economics (higher fuel and financing costs), potentially denting freight demand and delaying fleet replacement cycles — a direct headwind to the volume momentum management is guiding for.

fundamental.thesis: Polycab delivered strong sequential and year-on-year growth: Q4FY26 revenue of ₹6,787 cr is up ~30% YoY vs Q4FY25's ₹5,205 cr, and net profit of ₹773 cr is up ~6% YoY, with trailing four-quarter EPS adding up to roughly ₹180+, implying a business that is compounding well. However, rising crude oil (a key raw material input via copper and PVC) at $105 and a weakening INR at ~96 create meaningful margin pressure going forward, as commodity cost pass-through typically lags 1-2 quarters. The balance sheet and auditor filings appear clean with no red flags, but the stock sits in the Industrials/Materials-adjacent space which carries a sector underweight tilt in the current macro regime, moderating our overall conviction.

ONGC is India's dominant state-owned upstream oil and gas producer, contributing the majority of domestic crude and natural gas output, with significant downstream exposure via HPCL and MRPL subsidiaries. Over 9MFY26, revenue declined 6.2% YoY to ₹96,580 cr and PAT fell 10.0% YoY to ₹26,244 cr (PAT margin 27.2%), reflecting the adverse impact of lower crude realisations and subsidy-sharing — trajectory is clearly negative with no reversal signal yet visible in the available data. On valuation, trailing P/E of 9.51× sits well below the Energy sector median of 8–14× (near the lower bound), EV/EBITDA of 5.32× is undemanding for a capital-intensive upstream business, P/B of 0.98× implies the market values ONGC below book, and forward P/E of 6.79× signals the street expects meaningful earnings recovery — together these place ONGC firmly in 'cheap' territory on multiple metrics. The biggest risk is that rising crude at $93.65 is a double-edged sword for ONGC — higher realisations boost upstream profits but can simultaneously trigger government-mandated upstream subsidy contributions and downstream margin compression, which has historically eroded the headline upside.
